stem:
A 41-year-old man presents to his primary care provider with abdominal pain. He says that the pain comes and goes throughout the day and usually lasts 20-30 minutes per episode. He can point to the spot 1-2 inches above the umbilicus where he feels the pain. He denies any feeling of regurgitation or nighttime cough but endorses nausea. He reports that he used to eat three large meals per day but has found that eating more frequently improves his pain. He tried a couple pills of ibuprofen with food over the past couple days and thinks it helped. He has gained four pounds since his past appointment three months ago. The patient denies any diarrhea or change in his stools. He has no past medical history. He drinks 5-6 beers on the weekend and has a 20 pack-year smoking history. He denies any family history of cancer. On physical exam, he is tender to palpation above the umbilicus. Bowel sounds are present. A stool guaiac test is positive. The patient undergoes endoscopy with biopsy to diagnose his condition.

Which of the following is most likely to be found on histology?
option_A: Noncaseating granulomas in the small intestine
option_C: Urease-producing organism in the small intestine
option_D: PAS-positive material in the small intestine
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "B",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "rationale": "The patient's presentation of epigastric pain relieved by eating, along with risk factors like smoking and NSAID use, strongly suggests peptic ulcer disease. A positive stool guaiac test indicates bleeding, a common complication. Endoscopy with biopsy would most likely reveal a mucosal defect, which is the hallmark of an ulcer."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The patient's presentation of epigastric pain relieved by eating, along with risk factors like smoking and NSAID use, strongly suggests peptic ulcer disease. A positive stool guaiac test indicates bleeding, a common complication. Endoscopy with biopsy would most likely reveal a mucosal defect, which is the hallmark of an ulcer.

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to a pediatrician by his parents for evaluation of frequent bed wetting during the night. A detailed history reveals that there has been no history of urinary incontinence during the day since the boy was 4 years of age, but that he has never been dry at night continuously for 1 week. There is no history of urinary tract infections, urgency, frequency, or hesitancy. On physical examination, the boys vital signs are stable. His neurologic and abdominal examinations are completely normal. His laboratory investigations are as follows:
Urine-specific gravity (first-morning sample) 1.035
Urine red blood cells Absent
Urine pus cells Absent
Urine culture Negative
Which of the following is the next step in the management of this patient?
option_A: Ultrasonogram of the urinary bladder and kidneys
option_B: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine
option_D: Reassuring the parents and use of an enuresis alarm
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "D",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.95,
  "rationale": "The patient has primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis, as evidenced by never being dry at night and having no daytime symptoms or signs of underlying pathology. The normal physical exam and urinalysis rule out common organic causes. Therefore, first-line management involves behavioral interventions like an enuresis alarm and parental reassurance."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The patient has primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis, as evidenced by never being dry at night and having no daytime symptoms or signs of underlying pathology. The normal physical exam and urinalysis rule out common organic causes. Therefore, first-line management involves behavioral interventions like an enuresis alarm and parental reassurance.

stem: A 17-year-old girl is referred by her dentist for a suspected eating disorder. She has been visiting the same dentist since childhood and for the past 2 years has had at least 2 visits for dental caries. She eventually admitted to him that she regularly induces vomiting by putting her fingers down her throat. She says she has been doing this for the last few years and purging at least once a week. More recently, she has been inducing emesis more often and even looked into diuretics as she feels that she is gaining more and more weight compared to her skinny friends. Her BMI is at the 50th percentile for her age and sex. Which of the following features is most consistent with this patients condition?
option_A: Patients can have a history of both anorexia and bulimia
option_B: Patients usually have significant medical complications
option_C: Patients with this disorder are not further sub-typed
option_D: Patients do not usually initiate treatment
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "A",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "rationale": "The patient's presentation is classic for bulimia nervosa, characterized by recurrent binge eating and compensatory behaviors (purging) while maintaining a normal or above-normal weight. It is well-documented that a significant number of individuals with bulimia nervosa have a past history of anorexia nervosa, a phenomenon known as diagnostic crossover."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The patient's presentation is classic for bulimia nervosa, characterized by recurrent binge eating and compensatory behaviors (purging) while maintaining a normal or above-normal weight. It is well-documented that a significant number of individuals with bulimia nervosa have a past history of anorexia nervosa, a phenomenon known as diagnostic crossover.

stem: A previously healthy 18-month-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents for evaluation of an abdominal mass noticed a few days ago. He appears markedly pale and lethargic. Examination shows a 6-cm, nonmobile mass in the left upper quadrant that crosses the midline. 24-hour urine collection shows elevated homovanillic acid and vanillylmandelic acid. Further evaluation including biopsy confirms the diagnosis of intermediate-risk neuroblastoma. The physician recommends the established standard treatment, which is initiation of neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by surgical resection, if possible. After a thorough discussion of the risks and benefits of chemotherapy, the likelihood of unresectability without neoadjuvant treatment, and the prognosis without it, the patient's parents steadily refuse chemotherapy because they do not want their son to suffer the side effects. They prefer to take their son home for supportive care only. Which of the following is the most appropriate action by the physician?
option_A: Help the parents to arrange supportive care at home
option_B: Recommend for parents to take 2 weeks to think about decision
option_C: Seek a court order for neoadjuvant chemotherapy
option_D: Attempt surgical resection of the tumor
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "C",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.95,
  "rationale": "The parents are refusing a standard, life-saving treatment for their child's serious but treatable condition. Since the child cannot consent and the parents' decision places the child at significant risk of harm (death), the physician has an ethical and legal obligation to act in the child's best interest. Seeking a court order is the appropriate step to ensure the child receives necessary medical care."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The parents are refusing a standard, life-saving treatment for their child's serious but treatable condition. Since the child cannot consent and the parents' decision places the child at significant risk of harm (death), the physician has an ethical and legal obligation to act in the child's best interest. Seeking a court order is the appropriate step to ensure the child receives necessary medical care.

stem: An otherwise healthy 50-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of increasingly frequent episodes of upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. He has had a 3.2-kg (7-lb) weight loss during this time. Physical examination shows bilateral pitting pedal edema. An endoscopy shows prominent rugae in the gastric fundus. Biopsy shows parietal cell atrophy. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "B",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.95,
  "rationale": "The patient's symptoms of abdominal pain, diarrhea, and weight loss, combined with endoscopic findings of prominent gastric rugae and biopsy showing parietal cell atrophy (a late finding after chronic hyperstimulation), strongly suggest Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. This condition is caused by a gastrin-secreting tumor (gastrinoma), which is often ectopic. The pedal edema is likely due to protein-losing enteropathy from the severe mucosal damage."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The patient's symptoms of abdominal pain, diarrhea, and weight loss, combined with endoscopic findings of prominent gastric rugae and biopsy showing parietal cell atrophy (a late finding after chronic hyperstimulation), strongly suggest Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. This condition is caused by a gastrin-secreting tumor (gastrinoma), which is often ectopic. The pedal edema is likely due to protein-losing enteropathy from the severe mucosal damage.

stem: A 29-year-old primigravid woman at 35 weeks' gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. She has no history of serious medical illness. She has had an uncomplicated pregnancy. Her last ultrasound at 22 weeks' gestation was normal. On admission, fetal heartbeats cannot be detected by fetal doppler monitor. Ultrasound shows decreased amniotic fluid levels and no evidence of fetal movement, respiration, or heart activity. The patient gives birth to a 2296 g (5 lb 1 oz) male infant. Physical examination shows no signs of life. There are no visible malformations. The placenta is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
option_A: Obtain consent for parental genetic testing
option_B: Obtain photographs, x-ray imaging, and MRI
option_D: Perform maternal antibody and thrombophilia testing
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "C",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "rationale": "Autopsy is the single most useful investigation in determining the cause of stillbirth, identifying abnormalities in up to 30-50% of cases. It provides crucial information for counseling the parents about the cause and recurrence risk. Other investigations like imaging and genetic testing are also important but are typically performed in conjunction with, or guided by, autopsy findings."
}
```
gemini_rationale: Autopsy is the single most useful investigation in determining the cause of stillbirth, identifying abnormalities in up to 30-50% of cases. It provides crucial information for counseling the parents about the cause and recurrence risk. Other investigations like imaging and genetic testing are also important but are typically performed in conjunction with, or guided by, autopsy findings.

stem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to his pediatrician due to a left breast lump under his nipple. He noticed it last month and felt that it has increased slightly in size. It is tender to touch but has no overlying skin changes. There is no breast discharge. The patient has cryptorchidism as an infant and underwent a successful orchiopexy. In addition, he was recently diagnosed with ADHD and is currently on methylphenidate with improvement in his symptoms. He has a family history of type I diabetes in his father and breast fibroadenoma in his mother. On exam, the patient is at the 82nd percentile for height, 79th percentile for weight, and 80th percentile for BMI. He has tanner IV pubic hair. Testicular volume is 7 mL on each side, and both testes are smooth and freely mobile. Breast exam shows a normal right breast and a 3-centimeter round, firm, and slightly tender discrete mass under the left nipple. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of this patients condition?

```json
  "choice": "D",

  "rationale": "The patient is a 13-year-old boy in mid-puberty (Tanner IV pubic hair, testicular volume 7 mL) presenting with a tender, firm, subareolar mass. This clinical picture is classic for pubertal gynecomastia, which is a benign enlargement of glandular breast tissue. The unilateral presentation is common, and it often resolves spontaneously."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The patient is a 13-year-old boy in mid-puberty (Tanner IV pubic hair, testicular volume 7 mL) presenting with a tender, firm, subareolar mass. This clinical picture is classic for pubertal gynecomastia, which is a benign enlargement of glandular breast tissue. The unilateral presentation is common, and it often resolves spontaneously.

stem: A 47-year-old executive schedules an appointment his physician for a routine medical check-up. He currently has no complaints and claims to be as fit as a fiddle. The physical examination findings are unremarkable, except for a mid-systolic murmur heard in the 2nd left intercostal space that radiates to the carotids on auscultation. The physician instructs the patient to stand from a supine position with the stethoscope still placed on his chest. Which of the following changes would occur with this maneuver?
option_A: An increase in pulmonary capillary wedge pressure
option_B: A reduction in the slope of the pacemaker potential
option_C: An increase in left ventricular end-diastolic pressure
option_D: An increase in right atrial pressure
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "B",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "rationale": "Standing from a supine position causes venous pooling in the lower extremities, which decreases venous return to the heart. This drop in preload triggers a baroreceptor reflex, leading to increased sympathetic and decreased parasympathetic tone. The increased sympathetic stimulation to the sinoatrial (SA) node increases the slope of the pacemaker potential, resulting in reflex tachycardia."
}
```
gemini_rationale: Standing from a supine position causes venous pooling in the lower extremities, which decreases venous return to the heart. This drop in preload triggers a baroreceptor reflex, leading to increased sympathetic and decreased parasympathetic tone. The increased sympathetic stimulation to the sinoatrial (SA) node increases the slope of the pacemaker potential, resulting in reflex tachycardia.

stem: An 83-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with fever, weakness, and decreased responsiveness. He is diagnosed with urosepsis based on urinalysis and culture and started on ceftriaxone and intravenous fluids. By hospital day 3, he is clinically improving. During the evening, the patient becomes irritable. He is talking to someone despite nobody being present in the room. He is easily agitated and attempts to strike a nurse with a remote control to his TV. Subsequently, the patient keeps getting out of bed and trying to walk away despite being a fall risk. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "A",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "rationale": "The patient is presenting with acute delirium with agitation and aggression, a common complication in elderly hospitalized patients with infection. First-line management involves non-pharmacologic interventions, but when these fail or the patient is a danger to themselves or others, a low-dose atypical antipsychotic like olanzapine is the preferred pharmacologic agent. Benzodiazepines like lorazepam can worsen delirium in the elderly, and physical restraints are a last resort."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The patient is presenting with acute delirium with agitation and aggression, a common complication in elderly hospitalized patients with infection. First-line management involves non-pharmacologic interventions, but when these fail or the patient is a danger to themselves or others, a low-dose atypical antipsychotic like olanzapine is the preferred pharmacologic agent. Benzodiazepines like lorazepam can worsen delirium in the elderly, and physical restraints are a last resort.

His temperature is 38.0C (100.4F), pulse is 65/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 140/78 mm Hg. Diffuse crackles are heard over bibasilar lung fields. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count 16,000/mm3
Platelet count 150,000/mm3
Serum
Na+ 131 mEq/L
Cl-
102 mEq/L
K+ 4.7 mEq/L
HCO3- 26 mEq/L

stem: A newborn is found to be extremely cyanotic immediately after birth. He then develops progressive respiratory failure and is admitted to the neonatal ICU. The machine-like murmur likely represents a patent ductus arteriosus, which is a necessary shunt for survival in this condition, and the atrial septostomy is a life-saving palliative procedure to improve mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood."
```json
}
```

The other options would either worsen the symptoms or are not the most appropriate first-line treatment for this specific adverse effect."
```json
}
```

stem: A scientist is studying the properties of myosin-actin interactions in a sample of human muscle tissue. She has identified a drug that selectively inhibits phosphate release by the myosin head. If she gives this drug to a sample of human muscle tissue under physiologic conditions, which of the following steps in cross-bridge cycling will most likely be blocked?
option_A: Exposure of myosin-binding sites on actin
gemini_raw:
```json
{
  "choice": "D",
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.95,
  "rationale": "The release of inorganic phosphate (Pi) from the myosin head is the direct trigger for the power stroke, where the myosin head pivots and pulls the actin filament. Inhibiting Pi release would cause the myosin head to remain in a high-energy, weakly-bound state to actin, thus preventing the power stroke from occurring."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The release of inorganic phosphate (Pi) from the myosin head is the direct trigger for the power stroke, where the myosin head pivots and pulls the actin filament. Inhibiting Pi release would cause the myosin head to remain in a high-energy, weakly-bound state to actin, thus preventing the power stroke from occurring.

A 70-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department for the evaluation of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting for 1 day. Computed tomography shows a small bowel perforation. The patient is prepared for emergent exploratory laparotomy. She is sedated with midazolam, induced with propofol, intubated, and maintained on nitrous oxide and isoflurane for the duration of the surgery. A single perforation in the terminal ileum is diagnosed intraoperatively and successfully repaired. The patient is transferred to the intensive care unit. The ventilator is set at an FiO2 of 50%, tidal volume of 1000 mL, respiratory rate of 12/min, and positive end-expiratory pressure of 2.5 cm H2O. Her temperature is 37.3C (99.1F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 111/50 mm Hg. She is responsive to painful stimuli. Lung examination shows bilateral rales. Abdominal examination shows a distended abdomen and intact abdominal surgical incisions. The remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormalities. Arterial blood gas analysis shows:
pH 7.44
pO2 54 mm Hg
pCO2 31 mm Hg
HCO3- 22 mm Hg
Which of the following is the best next step in the management of this patient?"

Increasing PEEP is the most appropriate next step to improve oxygenation by recruiting collapsed alveoli and improving V/Q matching."
```json
}
```

stem: A 24-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department complaining of painful contractions occurring every 10 minutes for the past 2 hours, consistent with latent labor. Her sexually transmitted infections status is unknown. As part of the patients workup, she undergoes a series of rapid screening tests that result in the administration of zidovudine during delivery. Which of the following is most true about the confirmatory test?
option_A: It is an HIV-1/HIV2 antibody differentiation immunoassay
option_B: It determines the genotype of the virus
option_C: It is a Northern blot, identifying the presence of DNA
option_D: It is a Northern blot, identifying the presence of RNA

```json
  "abstain_code": null,
  "confidence": 0.95,
  "rationale": "The current CDC-recommended algorithm for HIV diagnosis uses a rapid screening test (4th generation antigen/antibody combination immunoassay) followed by a confirmatory HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation immunoassay. This confirmatory test distinguishes between HIV-1 and HIV-2 antibodies. Western blot is no longer the recommended confirmatory test."
}
```
gemini_rationale: The current CDC-recommended algorithm for HIV diagnosis uses a rapid screening test (4th generation antigen/antibody combination immunoassay) followed by a confirmatory HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation immunoassay. This confirmatory test distinguishes between HIV-1 and HIV-2 antibodies. Western blot is no longer the recommended confirmatory test.
